description: Figure 21 presents the tensile properties and work hardening behavior of Fe-Al steels as a function of aluminum content (0–25 wt%), illustrating the influence of crystal structure ordering on mechanical performance. Panel (a) displays yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), uniform elongation (UE), and total elongation (TE) versus Al content across different phase regions (A2, K1, A2+DO3, DO3, B2). The YS increases with Al up to ~13 wt% (~575 MPa) then drops sharply at 13–15% before stabilizing, while UTS rises continuously. Both UE and TE decrease with increasing Al, with a ductile-to-brittle transition occurring at 8–10% Al. Panel (b) shows the work hardening rate measured between 1–1.5% plastic strain, which remains low (<2 GPa) for disordered alloys (<7% Al), peaks sharply at ~16% Al (16 GPa) corresponding to ordered DO3 phase formation, and decreases moderately above 20% Al. These curves demonstrate that solid solution strengthening by Al provides ~40 MPa/wt% increase in ferrite, but ordering phenomena dominate the strain hardening response through superdislocation shearing of ordered phases.
